case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: User-provided full protocol with 15 min batch time, 83% isolated yield, openai council benchmark.

streams.pump_role: single feed containing all reagents premixed in DES
streams.reasoning: From Chemistry Plan stream_logic: All components premixed in single stream. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate stable together at RT; reaction only initiates at 120°C. Premixing ensures dipolarophile present when transient nitrile oxide generated, preventing dimerization. DES viscosity requires single stream to minimize pumping challenges.

pre_council_proposal.mixing_order_reasoning: From Chemistry Plan: Single premixed feed eliminates need for mixing. All reagents must be co-present when heated because nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and will dimerize if not immediately trapped by dipolarophile.
post_reactor_steps: cooling to room temperature to stop reaction
pre_council_proposal.chemistry_notes: Thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition proceeding via in-situ nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate at 120°C, followed by [3+2] cycloaddition with phenylacetylene. Rate-limiting step is thermal dehydration/tautomerization to form transient nitrile oxide. DES provides hydrogen-bonding environment facilitating nitrile oxide formation. High temperature (120°C) and 60 min residence time from analogies ensure complete conversion. BPR required to prevent DES component volatilization. Stainless steel tubing chosen for thermal conductivity and DES compatibility.

reasoning_per_field.residence_time_min: Calculation estimated 60.0 min (range 1.0-90.0 min). Analogy 2 used 20 min for [3+2] cycloaddition but at lower temperature (60°C vs 120°C). Using 60 min to ensure complete nitrile oxide generation and cycloaddition at our conditions.
reasoning_per_field.flow_rate_mL_min: Calculation: V_R/τ = 30.0 mL / 60.0 min = 0.5 mL/min. Verified against analogies - Analogy 3 used 0.12 mL/min for high-temperature process, our rate is reasonable for DES viscosity.
reasoning_per_field.temperature_C: Same as batch (120°C) - required for thermal nitrile oxide generation. Chemistry Plan confirms this is critical temperature for rate-limiting dehydration step.
reasoning_per_field.concentration_M: Calculation result: 0.0667 M (diluted from batch 0.5 M due to flow rate constraints). Lower than Analogy 1 (0.5 M) but adequate for this thermal process.
reasoning_per_field.BPR_bar: Calculation required 5.0 bar. Analogy 3 used 47.6 bar for 154°C operation. Our 5 bar sufficient for 120°C to prevent DES component volatilization and maintain liquid phase.
reasoning_per_field.reactor_type: Coil reactor from Chemistry Plan stage 1. Analogies 1 and 3 confirm coil/tubular reactors suitable for thermal processes.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_material: Stainless steel for thermal conductivity at 120°C and DES compatibility. Analogy 1 used steel coil successfully. Better heat transfer than PFA for this thermal process.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_ID_mm: 1.0 mm from calculation (d = 1.00 mm). Balances heat transfer with manageable pressure drop for viscous DES. Calculation confirmed Re = 10.6 (laminar flow).
reasoning_per_field.reactor_volume_mL: 30.0 mL from calculation (V_R = 30.00 mL). Matches Analogy 1 volume, appropriate for 60 min residence time.
reasoning_per_field.wavelength_nm: null - Chemistry Plan confirms purely thermal reaction, no photochemical activation involved.
reasoning_per_field.deoxygenation_method: null - Chemistry Plan confirms not oxygen sensitive, thermal pericyclic mechanism unaffected by O2.

literature_analogies: Analogy 3: high-temperature cyclization in tubular reactor

chemistry_plan.reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
chemistry_plan.bond_formed: C–O and C–C bonds in isoxazole ring
chemistry_plan.bond_broken: N–H (tautomerization/dehydration of ethyl nitroacetate to nitrile oxide)

feed_streams.reasoning: All reagents are premixed in DES; nitrile oxide must be generated in the presence of the dipolarophile to avoid dimerization. No incompatibility between phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate at room temperature — reaction only proceeds at elevated temperature. Single feed stream simplifies pumping of viscous DES.

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates nitrile oxide in situ via thermal dehydration/tautomerization; 2 equiv used to drive reaction to completion

reagents.name: tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB)
reagents.notes: Part of deep eutectic solvent system; acts as reaction medium and may facilitate nitrile oxide generation

reagents.name: ethylene glycol (EG)
reagents.notes: Part of deep eutectic solvent system

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to its aci-nitro form (nitronic acid), facilitated by the DES hydrogen-bonding environment at 120°C

Dehydration of the aci-nitro tautomer to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (the reactive 1,3-dipole). Water is lost. This step is rate-limiting as it requires thermal activation to form the transient nitrile oxide.

Concerted [3+2] cycloaddition of ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide with phenylacetylene. The nitrile oxide acts as the 1,3-dipole and phenylacetylene as the dipolarophile, forming the isoxazole ring regioselectively (5-phenyl regioisomer favored).

stream_logic.reasoning: All components premixed in a single stream. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate are stable together at room temperature; the reaction only initiates at elevated temperature (120°C). Premixing ensures the dipolarophile is present when the transient nitrile oxide is generated, preventing nitrile oxide dimerization to furoxan. DES is viscous (~50-100 cP at RT) so a single stream minimizes mixing challenges. Concentration: phenylacetylene 0.5 M, ethyl nitroacetate 1.0 M in 1 mL DES.

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Single premixed feed. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate must be co-present when heated because the nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and will dimerize to furoxan if not immediately trapped by the dipolarophile. No catalyst, base, or additive is needed, so there is no incompatibility concern at room temperature.
chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species or radicals involved; thermal pericyclic mechanism is unaffected by O2.
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: Reaction reaches full conversion in 15 min at 120°C. In flow, simply cooling the output stream to room temperature effectively stops the reaction. Batch protocol uses water quench for workup/extraction purposes only, not to terminate a dangerous reaction.
chemistry_plan.wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism (well-established nitrile oxide cycloaddition). The DES viscosity (~50-100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) may require careful pump selection (e.g., syringe pump or HPLC pump rated for viscous media). Back-pressure regulator recommended to keep system pressurized above boiling point of any volatile components at 120°C. The 15 min residence time at 120°C is straightforward for a heated coil reactor. Coil ID of 0.75-1.0 mm recommended to balance heat transfer with manageable pressure drop given DES viscosity. Stainless steel coil preferred for thermal conductivity. Uncertainty: exact viscosity of TBAB/EG at operating temperature; may need to verify pumpability experimentally.

final_design_candidate.human_explanation:
## Flow Chemistry Design for 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition

### (1) Proposed Flow Setup

**Single-stream thermal coil reactor:**
- Feed: All reagents premixed in TBAB/EG (1:5) deep eutectic solvent
  - Phenylacetylene (1.0 equiv, 0.0667 M)
  - Ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv)
  - TBAB/EG DES as reaction medium
- Reactor: Stainless steel coil, 0.75 mm ID, 3.534 mL volume
- Conditions: 120.0°C, 60.0 min residence time, 0.0589 mL/min flow rate
- Back-pressure regulator: 5.0 bar
- Post-reactor: Cooling to room temperature

### (2) Rationale for Conditions

**Temperature (120°C):** Required for thermal generation of nitrile oxide from ethyl nitroacetate via dehydration/tautomerization - this is the rate-limiting step. Matches batch protocol temperature.

**Residence time (60 min):** Extended from typical flow cycloadditions (e.g., 20 min at 60°C in similar [3+2] systems) to ensure complete nitrile oxide formation and cycloaddition at our conditions. The transient nitrile oxide intermediate requires sufficient time for generation and trapping.

**Single premixed stream:** Critical design choice - the nitrile oxide intermediate is highly reactive and will dimerize to furoxan if not immediately trapped by the dipolarophile. Premixing ensures phenylacetylene is present when nitrile oxide forms at temperature.

**DES medium:** The hydrogen-bonding environment facilitates nitrile oxide generation. Viscosity (~50-100 cP) necessitates careful pump selection but enables single-stream operation.

**Stainless steel tubing:** Chosen for thermal conductivity at 120°C and compatibility with DES, following successful precedent in thermal flow processes.

### (3) Key Differences from Batch

- **Concentration reduced:** From 0.5 M to 0.0667 M due to flow rate constraints with viscous DES
- **No mixing step:** Batch requires heating separate components; flow uses premixed feed to prevent nitrile oxide dimerization
- **Continuous cooling:** Replaces batch quench - reaction stops upon cooling, no water quench needed
- **Pressurized system:** 5.0 bar BPR prevents DES component volatilization at 120°C

### (4) Engineering Validation Warnings

⚠️ **Scoring system failure noted** - domain expert validation returned default scores only. The design is based on geometric optimization without full engineering validation.

**Pump considerations:** DES viscosity may require syringe pump or HPLC pump rated for viscous media. Verify pumpability experimentally.

**Heat transfer:** 0.75 mm ID balances thermal conductivity with manageable pressure drop, but verify temperature uniformity across coil length.

**Conversion estimate:** 63% predicted conversion - may need optimization if higher yields required.

**Confidence: LOW** — The closest literature analogy has a similarity score of 0.34 (threshold for MEDIUM: 0.50). No close precedent found in the corpus for this exact reaction class and conditions. Treat this proposal as a starting hypothesis requiring careful experimental validation before scale-up.

- ⛔ Candidate 24 (τ=35.9 min, d=1.6 mm): dual-criterion mixing fail: Da_mass=1.64 > 1.0 AND r_mix=0.297 > 0.20; X=0.45 < X_minimum=0.50 (insufficient conversion)

formatted_result.explanation:
## Flow Chemistry Design for 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition

### (1) Proposed Flow Setup

**Single-stream thermal coil reactor:**
- Feed: All reagents premixed in TBAB/EG (1:5) deep eutectic solvent
  - Phenylacetylene (1.0 equiv, 0.0667 M)
  - Ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv)
  - TBAB/EG DES as reaction medium
- Reactor: Stainless steel coil, 0.75 mm ID, 3.534 mL volume
- Conditions: 120.0°C, 60.0 min residence time, 0.0589 mL/min flow rate
- Back-pressure regulator: 5.0 bar
- Post-reactor: Cooling to room temperature

### (2) Rationale for Conditions

**Temperature (120°C):** Required for thermal generation of nitrile oxide from ethyl nitroacetate via dehydration/tautomerization - this is the rate-limiting step. Matches batch protocol temperature.

**Residence time (60 min):** Extended from typical flow cycloadditions (e.g., 20 min at 60°C in similar [3+2] systems) to ensure complete nitrile oxide formation and cycloaddition at our conditions. The transient nitrile oxide intermediate requires sufficient time for generation and trapping.

**Single premixed stream:** Critical design choice - the nitrile oxide intermediate is highly reactive and will dimerize to furoxan if not immediately trapped by the dipolarophile. Premixing ensures phenylacetylene is present when nitrile oxide forms at temperature.

**DES medium:** The hydrogen-bonding environment facilitates nitrile oxide generation. Viscosity (~50-100 cP) necessitates careful pump selection but enables single-stream operation.

**Stainless steel tubing:** Chosen for thermal conductivity at 120°C and compatibility with DES, following successful precedent in thermal flow processes.

### (3) Key Differences from Batch

- **Concentration reduced:** From 0.5 M to 0.0667 M due to flow rate constraints with viscous DES
- **No mixing step:** Batch requires heating separate components; flow uses premixed feed to prevent nitrile oxide dimerization
- **Continuous cooling:** Replaces batch quench - reaction stops upon cooling, no water quench needed
- **Pressurized system:** 5.0 bar BPR prevents DES component volatilization at 120°C

### (4) Engineering Validation Warnings

⚠️ **Scoring system failure noted** - domain expert validation returned default scores only. The design is based on geometric optimization without full engineering validation.

**Pump considerations:** DES viscosity may require syringe pump or HPLC pump rated for viscous media. Verify pumpability experimentally.

**Heat transfer:** 0.75 mm ID balances thermal conductivity with manageable pressure drop, but verify temperature uniformity across coil length.

**Conversion estimate:** 63% predicted conversion - may need optimization if higher yields required.

**Confidence: LOW** — The closest literature analogy has a similarity score of 0.34 (threshold for MEDIUM: 0.50). No close precedent found in the corpus for this exact reaction class and conditions. Treat this proposal as a starting hypothesis requiring careful experimental validation before scale-up.

The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).

Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate are stable together at room temperature; the reaction only initiates at elevated temperature (120°C). Premixing ensures the dipolarophile is present when the transient nitrile oxide is generated, preventing nitrile oxide dimerization to furoxan. DES is viscous (~50-100 cP at RT) so a single stream minimizes mixing challenges. Concentration: phenylacetylene 0.5 M, ethyl nitroacetate 1.0 M in 1 mL DES.

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Single premixed feed. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate must be co-present when heated because the nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and will dimerize to furoxan if not immediately trapped by the dipolarophile. No catalyst, base, or additive is needed, so there is no incompatibility concern at room temperature.
chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species or radicals involved; thermal pericyclic mechanism is unaffected by O2.
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: Reaction reaches full conversion in 15 min at 120°C. In flow, simply cooling the output stream to room temperature effectively stops the reaction. Batch protocol uses water quench for workup/extraction purposes only, not to terminate a dangerous reaction.
chemistry_plan.wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism (well-established nitrile oxide cycloaddition). The DES viscosity (~50-100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) may require careful pump selection (e.g., syringe pump or HPLC pump rated for viscous media). Back-pressure regulator recommended to keep system pressurized above boiling point of any volatile components at 120°C. The 15 min residence time at 120°C is straightforward for a heated coil reactor. Coil ID of 0.75-1.0 mm recommended to balance heat transfer with manageable pressure drop given DES viscosity. Stainless steel coil preferred for thermal conductivity. Uncertainty: exact viscosity of TBAB/EG at operating temperature; may need to verify pumpability experimentally.

analogies.summary: This photocycloaddition reaction utilizes triplet sensitization with 2,2'-dimethoxythioxanthone (2,2'-MeOTX) as the photocatalyst at 1 mol% loading under 455 nm COB LED irradiation. The flow process operates in acetonitrile at 200°C with a 5-minute residence time and 6.0 mL/min flow rate using a FlowSyn steel coil reactor (30 mL volume) equipped with a 34 bar back-pressure regulator. Flow chemistry enabled daisy-chaining of the initial photochemical step with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions, dramatically improving yield from 16% in batch (2 hours) to 88% in flow with a 24-fold intensification factor. The high-temperature operation required for the thermal cascade was much safer to implement in the continuous flow reactor compared to batch processing.

chemistry.reaction_name: [2 + 2] photocycloaddition followed by thermal electrocyclic cascade
chemistry.bond_formed: C-C bonds in cyclobutane ring and subsequent cascade products
chemistry.scope_summary: Five aryl enamide derivatives tested (phenyl, 3-F, 3-Br, 3-OMe, 4-CF3) with yields of 63-68% for most substrates. The 4-CF3 derivative was resistant to the thermal fragmentation step.

optimization_table.key_findings: MeCN solvent with 0.5 M concentration at 200°C with 5 min residence time gave optimal results

figures.key_information: photochemical reactor setup with dual light sources positioned above a round bottom flask on a heating plate

Figure 7 . The ease at which this could be purified by filtration represents one of the main benefits of acetonitrile over xylene, which would not be possible without the use of continuous flow technology. This result represents a 24 h productivity of 490 g. In conclusion, we have demonstrated a rare example of an excited-state photochemical reaction successfully daisy-chained with a thermal r | Figure 6. translation_logic.batch_limitation: Low yield (16%) and long reaction time (120 min) in batch mode
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Daisy-chaining photochemistry with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions enabled by flow technology, achieving high productivity
translation_logic.what_changed: Temperature increased to 200°C, residence time reduced to 5 min, back pressure of 34 bar applied
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Solvent (MeCN), concentration (0.5 M), light source power (36 W)
translation_logic.safety_improvement: High temperature thermal cascade reactions safer in flow than batch

figure_descriptions: Reactor scheme showing photochemical setup with light sources
Flow schematic for daisy-chained photo/thermal process

full_record.notes: High confidence extraction with clear quantitative data from optimization tables. Flow process shows dramatic improvement in yield (16% to 88%) and time reduction (120 min to 5 min). Productivity of 20 g/h mentioned in key claim.

analogies.summary: This process demonstrates a [3 + 2] dipolar cycloaddition reaction conducted in a continuous flow unit (CFU) with automated pumps. The reaction uses DMSO as solvent with diisopropylamine as base, operating at 60°C with a 20-minute residence time at 0.165 mL/min flow rate, achieving 96% yield. The flow process allows for higher temperature operation (60°C vs 20°C in batch) while maintaining excellent yield, with the batch baseline achieving 100% yield at room temperature in the same reaction time. The continuous flow setup enables better temperature control and process intensification, allowing the reaction to proceed efficiently at elevated temperature with only a minimal decrease in yield compared to the batch process.

chemistry.scope_summary: The process shows a wide scope for synthesizing 1-aryl-1,2,3-triazoles from various anilines, including electron-rich and electron-poor substrates, yielding triazoles efficiently in good yields.

optimization_table.key_findings: CuI (10 mol%) with 1,10-phenanthroline (10 mol%) at 60°C for 20 min gave 100% conversion.

full_record.notes: Flow process achieves high yield with optimized conditions, BPR inferred from reactor scheme.

analogies.record_id: Yang 등 - 2024 - Safety Guided Process Development and Scale-Up of the Highly Energetic Compound 4-Methyl-1,2,5-oxadi.pdf
analogies.summary: This process involves the synthesis of 4-methyl-1,2,5-oxadiazole, a highly energetic compound, using a nitration or cyclization reaction in a concentrated sulfuric acid/water mixture (63:6.3 ratio). The flow process was conducted in a tubular reactor at 154°C with a residence time of 179 minutes (approximately 3 hours) and a flow rate of 0.12 mL/min, requiring a back-pressure regulator at 47.6 bar to maintain liquid phase conditions. Flow chemistry enabled a significant temperature increase from 65°C in batch to 154°C, resulting in an improved yield from 70.8% to 78.8%. The primary advantage of flow over batch was enhanced safety when handling this highly energetic compound, as the continuous process allows for better control of the exothermic reaction and reduces the inventory of hazardous material at any given time. The high pressure and temperature conditions achievable in the flow reactor also contributed to the improved reaction efficiency.

additives.name: N-methyl morpholine
additives.role: forms stable NMM salt of the product

streams.pump_role: single feed containing all reagents premixed in DES
streams.reasoning: From Chemistry Plan stream_logic: All components premixed in single stream. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate stable together at RT; reaction only initiates at 120°C. Premixing ensures dipolarophile present when transient nitrile oxide generated, preventing dimerization. DES viscosity requires single stream to minimize pumping challenges.

proposal.mixing_order_reasoning: From Chemistry Plan: Single premixed feed eliminates need for mixing. All reagents must be co-present when heated because nitrile oxide intermediate is transient and will dimerize if not immediately trapped by dipolarophile.
post_reactor_steps: cooling to room temperature to stop reaction
proposal.chemistry_notes: Thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition proceeding via in-situ nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate at 120°C, followed by [3+2] cycloaddition with phenylacetylene. Rate-limiting step is thermal dehydration/tautomerization to form transient nitrile oxide. DES provides hydrogen-bonding environment facilitating nitrile oxide formation. High temperature (120°C) and 60 min residence time from analogies ensure complete conversion. BPR required to prevent DES component volatilization. Stainless steel tubing chosen for thermal conductivity and DES compatibility.

reasoning_per_field.residence_time_min: Calculation estimated 60.0 min (range 1.0-90.0 min). Analogy 2 used 20 min for [3+2] cycloaddition but at lower temperature (60°C vs 120°C). Using 60 min to ensure complete nitrile oxide generation and cycloaddition at our conditions.
reasoning_per_field.flow_rate_mL_min: Calculation: V_R/τ = 30.0 mL / 60.0 min = 0.5 mL/min. Verified against analogies - Analogy 3 used 0.12 mL/min for high-temperature process, our rate is reasonable for DES viscosity.
reasoning_per_field.temperature_C: Same as batch (120°C) - required for thermal nitrile oxide generation. Chemistry Plan confirms this is critical temperature for rate-limiting dehydration step.
reasoning_per_field.concentration_M: Calculation result: 0.0667 M (diluted from batch 0.5 M due to flow rate constraints). Lower than Analogy 1 (0.5 M) but adequate for this thermal process.
reasoning_per_field.BPR_bar: Calculation required 5.0 bar. Analogy 3 used 47.6 bar for 154°C operation. Our 5 bar sufficient for 120°C to prevent DES component volatilization and maintain liquid phase.
reasoning_per_field.reactor_type: Coil reactor from Chemistry Plan stage 1. Analogies 1 and 3 confirm coil/tubular reactors suitable for thermal processes.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_material: Stainless steel for thermal conductivity at 120°C and DES compatibility. Analogy 1 used steel coil successfully. Better heat transfer than PFA for this thermal process.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_ID_mm: 1.0 mm from calculation (d = 1.00 mm). Balances heat transfer with manageable pressure drop for viscous DES. Calculation confirmed Re = 10.6 (laminar flow).
reasoning_per_field.reactor_volume_mL: 30.0 mL from calculation (V_R = 30.00 mL). Matches Analogy 1 volume, appropriate for 60 min residence time.
reasoning_per_field.wavelength_nm: null - Chemistry Plan confirms purely thermal reaction, no photochemical activation involved.
reasoning_per_field.deoxygenation_method: null - Chemistry Plan confirms not oxygen sensitive, thermal pericyclic mechanism unaffected by O2.

literature_analogies: Analogy 3: high-temperature cyclization in tubular reactor
